_purpose: Material life profile for Great Plains farm families during the Dust Bowl era, 1920-1940. Captures the arc from 1920s wheat-boom prosperity through the catastrophic convergence of drought, dust storms, and Depression that destroyed the agricultural economy of the Southern Plains and drove mass migration westward.
region: Southern Great Plains — Oklahoma panhandle, southwestern Kansas, Texas panhandle, southeastern Colorado, northeastern New Mexico
population: Farm families on wheat and cotton land. A mix of original homesteaders and their children and grandchildren, plus tenant farmers and sharecroppers who had moved onto the plains during the wheat boom. Many had prospered substantially in the 1910s and 1920s when wartime demand and high prices drove the great plow-up of native grassland. By the 1930s, prolonged drought, catastrophic dust storms, collapsing commodity prices, and the Depression had destroyed the agricultural economy. An estimated 2.5 million people left the Plains states during the 1930s.

position_guidance.landowner: Default framing. The ancestor owned their land — the frame farmhouse, the wheat acreage, the car in the yard. They appear in deed records, mortgage records, and tax rolls. When the Dust Bowl hit, they lost property they owned. The arc from prosperity to ruin described in this file applies directly.
position_guidance.tenant_farmer: Adjust framing. The ancestor rented land for cash or a crop share. Material life during the 1920s boom was similar to owners, but they had no equity to lose — and no mortgage debt to trap them. When conditions collapsed, tenants could leave more easily than owners, who were tied to mortgaged land. Tenant farmers do not appear in deed records but do appear in census farm schedules as 'tenant.'
position_guidance.sharecropper: Significant adjustment. Sharecroppers on the Southern Plains (both white and Black) lived at the bottom of the agricultural hierarchy. Their material life during the 1920s was below the prosperity described in this file — smaller houses, fewer goods, more dependence on the landlord. When the Dust Bowl hit, they had the least to lose materially but the fewest resources to leave.
position_guidance.hired_hand: The ancestor worked for wages on someone else's farm. They appear in census as 'farm laborer' — possibly living in the employer's household or in a bunkhouse. During the Dust Bowl, hired hands were the first let go when the work disappeared. They were among the earliest migrants because they had the least tying them to the land.

narrative_hooks.landowner: The frame farmhouse {ancestor_name} built with wheat-boom money in the 1920s was {pronoun_possessive} — deed recorded, mortgage held, and every nail an investment in permanence on the Southern Plains.
narrative_hooks.tenant_farmer: {ancestor_name} rented land in {county} — farming someone else's wheat acreage during the boom years and owing no mortgage when the bust came. Tenants could walk away. Owners could not.
narrative_hooks.sharecropper: {ancestor_name} farmed on shares in {county} — below even the tenant farmers in the hierarchy of the Southern Plains, living in the landlord's house, working the landlord's land, and owning nothing the dust could take except the will to leave.
narrative_hooks.hired_hand: {ancestor_name} worked for wages on a {county} farm — a hired hand with no land, no crop share, and no reason to stay when the work dried up with the rain.

housing.narrative: By the 1920s, the soddie was a memory. Frame farmhouses — one or two stories, clapboard-sided, with a kitchen garden and windmill — dotted the plains, testimony to two decades of agricultural prosperity. Some families had added electricity from a Delco home plant. But the dust storms of the 1930s turned these houses into traps. Fine silt, finer than flour, seeped through every crack around windows, doors, and floorboards, depositing drifts of dust inside the house that had to be shoveled out each morning. Families hung wet sheets and towels over windows and stuffed rags into every gap, but nothing kept the dust out during a black blizzard.

details.description: The standard Southern Plains farmhouse of the 1920s was a one-to-two-story milled-lumber frame structure, typically four to six rooms, with a front porch, a kitchen garden, and outbuildings including a barn, chicken house, root cellar, and privy. Many had been built during the flush years of 1910-1925 when wheat profits allowed substantial construction. They represented the family's proof of permanence on the land.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 10-18

details.description: During dust storms, fine soil particles penetrated every opening in the house. Families reported finding a quarter-inch layer of dust on all surfaces after a single storm. Dishes were stored upside down, food was covered with cloths, and beds were remade just before sleeping. Women swept and shoveled dust out of the house daily, sometimes multiple times per day. Caroline Henderson, a panhandle farmer, wrote that dust 'filters in through every crack and crevice' despite all efforts.
details.source: Egan, 'The Worst Hard Time,' pp. 152-158

details.description: Families tacked wet sheets, dampened towels, and moistened newspaper over windows and doorframes to filter dust from incoming air. During prolonged storms lasting two to three days, the sheets dried out and had to be rewetted constantly. Some families sealed rooms entirely with tape and paste, retreating to a single interior room during the worst storms. Red Cross distributed commercial dust masks, but many people improvised with damp cloth tied over the face.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 33-38

details.description: As families left — driven out by debt, drought, and despair — their abandoned houses filled with dust and tumbleweeds. Fences disappeared under drifts of soil. Barns collapsed. By 1935, entire communities in the Oklahoma and Texas panhandles were depopulated, the landscape reverting to a barren wasteland of drifting dunes. Remaining families watched their neighbors' houses go dark one by one.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 48-55

food_and_cooking.narrative: In the 1920s, the Southern Plains farmstead table was abundant. Women maintained large kitchen gardens, canned hundreds of quarts of vegetables and fruit each summer, raised chickens for eggs and meat, milked cows, and sold cream and eggs in town for cash. The cellar held potatoes, onions, and shelves of Mason jars. By the mid-1930s, this self-sufficient food system had collapsed. Gardens failed without rain. Livestock starved or were slaughtered by federal agents under the Emergency Cattle Purchase Program. Dust contaminated stored food and water. Families subsisted on government relief commodities — beans, flour, lard, canned beef — and whatever they could improvise, including jackrabbit from community rabbit drives and Russian thistle boiled as greens.

details.description: A prosperous plains farm wife in the 1920s canned 300 to 800 quarts of food per summer — tomatoes, green beans, corn, peaches, plums, pickles, jams, and jellies. Chickens provided eggs for the family and a cash surplus sold at the general store. Cream was separated and sold to the local creamery. Hog-killing in late autumn produced salt pork, hams, sausage, and lard for the year. This was not poverty — it was working agricultural prosperity.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 8-14

details.description: By 1932-1934, sustained drought and blowing dust had destroyed the capacity to grow food. Gardens could not germinate without moisture. Even when plants survived, dust storms buried or shredded them. Wells went dry or produced alkaline water. Women who had been self-sufficient food producers became dependent on government relief for the first time in their lives, a source of profound shame and anguish documented in letters and oral histories.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 100-110

details.description: Federal surplus commodities distributed through county relief offices included dried beans, flour, cornmeal, lard, canned beef, rice, and prunes. Red Cross also distributed food. Families lined up at distribution points, often traveling miles to collect rations. The commodities were monotonous but prevented starvation. Some families survived for years on little else. Local newspapers published recipes for making palatable meals from relief supplies.
details.source: Gregory, 'American Exodus,' pp. 11-17

details.description: As crops disappeared, jackrabbit populations exploded into plague proportions, devouring whatever vegetation remained. Communities organized rabbit drives — lines of men, women, and children driving thousands of rabbits into pens where they were clubbed to death. The drives served dual purposes: pest control and a source of meat. Some drives killed 6,000 rabbits in a single day. The meat was consumed, distributed to the needy, or shipped out.
details.source: Egan, 'The Worst Hard Time,' pp. 172-176

details.item: Russian thistle as feed and food
details.description: Russian thistle — tumbleweed — became a desperation feed for surviving livestock after all grass and hay were gone. County extension agents published instructions for cutting green tumbleweed before it dried, stacking it like hay, and feeding it to cattle. Some families boiled young tumbleweed shoots as greens for human consumption. The plant, itself an invasive species, became a symbol of the Dust Bowl landscape.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 55-59

clothing.narrative: Through the 1920s, plains farm families dressed respectably from the Sears or Montgomery Ward catalog and from shops in town. Women owned several housedresses and a good dress for church. Men had work clothes and a suit. Children were outfitted for school each fall. The 1930s destroyed this. When clothing wore out, it could not be replaced. Women remade adult garments into children's clothes, patched overalls until the patches had patches, and sewed dresses from cotton flour sacks and feed sacks printed with patterns by manufacturers who understood the need. Red Cross distributed relief clothing. Dust masks — commercial or improvised from damp cloth — became a daily necessity that defined the era's visual identity.

details.description: During the prosperous 1920s, farm families ordered clothing from Sears, Roebuck and Montgomery Ward catalogs or purchased from stores in county-seat towns. Women's housedresses cost 50 cents to $2. Men's overalls cost $1 to $2. A man's suit — essential for church, funerals, and business — cost $10 to $25. Shoes were store-bought. This was a materially comfortable, if not lavish, standard of dress.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 12-15

details.description: As cash disappeared, women turned to flour sacks and cotton feed sacks as fabric sources. Manufacturers responded by printing sacks with floral and geometric patterns, understanding that rural women would select feed brands based partly on fabric appeal. A dress required two to four sacks. The practice was universal, not shameful — nearly every farm child in the Depression-era plains wore feed-sack clothing at some point.
details.source: Gregory, 'American Exodus,' pp. 22-25

details.description: During dust storms, breathing unfiltered air could be lethal. Families improvised masks from damp washcloths, handkerchiefs, or strips of sheeting tied over the nose and mouth. Red Cross distributed commercial gauze masks. Children wore masks to walk to school. Photographs from the period show families, including small children, wearing masks as routine equipment. Wet masks clogged with mud-like paste within minutes during severe storms.
details.source: Egan, 'The Worst Hard Time,' pp. 195-200

details.description: Shoes, which could not be homemade, became an acute problem when cash vanished. Children's shoes wore through and were repaired with cardboard insoles and wire. Some children attended school barefoot into November. Adults resoled shoes with rubber cut from old tires. Relief agencies distributed used shoes, but sizes rarely matched. Going without shoes on ground hot enough to blister feet in summer and frozen in winter was a documented reality for the poorest families.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 120-125

hygiene_and_health.narrative: The health crisis of the Dust Bowl was as devastating as the economic one. 'Dust pneumonia' — silicosis caused by inhaling fine soil particles — sickened thousands and killed hundreds, especially children and the elderly. Hospitals in the panhandle towns were overwhelmed. Eye infections from blowing grit were endemic. The mental health toll was immense and documented in government reports: depression, anxiety, hopelessness, and a pervasive dread that the storms would never end. Static electricity generated by dust storms was so intense it knocked people down, shorted out automobile ignitions, and made it dangerous to shake hands or touch a metal fence.

details.description: Inhalation of fine silica particles caused a form of silicosis that physicians called 'dust pneumonia.' Symptoms included persistent cough, chest pain, difficulty breathing, and fever. Children and elderly were most vulnerable. Hospitals in Guymon, Liberal, and Boise City recorded hundreds of cases. Some victims — many of them children — died. Families sealed children into interior rooms and covered their faces with damp cloth, but exposure was unavoidable during storms lasting days.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 18-24

details.description: Blowing dust caused widespread conjunctivitis, corneal abrasions, and eye infections across the Southern Plains. Cattle went blind from dust exposure. Humans suffered constant eye irritation that made outdoor work agonizing. Goggles were improvised or purchased when available. County health nurses distributed eye wash and boric acid solution. Some residents developed permanent eye damage from years of exposure to wind-driven grit.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 40-44

details.description: Government reports and oral histories document widespread psychological distress among Dust Bowl families. Depression, anxiety, insomnia, and a sense of helplessness pervaded communities watching their world disintegrate. Women, isolated on failing farms with sick children and absent or despairing husbands, bore a disproportionate burden. Suicide rates in the worst-hit counties rose. Some families simply could not endure the unrelenting combination of poverty, dust, heat, and hopelessness.
details.source: Gregory, 'American Exodus,' pp. 30-36

details.description: Dust storms generated massive static charges. People received shocks from touching doorknobs, fence wire, or each other severe enough to knock them to the ground. Automobile engines stalled as static shorted out ignition systems; drivers dragged chains from their bumpers to ground the charge. Barbed-wire fences glowed with St. Elmo's fire during storms. The static added a surreal, almost supernatural quality to an already apocalyptic experience.
details.source: Egan, 'The Worst Hard Time,' pp. 214-220

details.description: Doctors in plains towns operated on a cash basis, and as the farm economy collapsed, medical care became unaffordable. County health nurses — funded by state and federal agencies — made rounds to isolated farms, treating dust pneumonia cases, distributing masks and eye wash, and documenting the health crisis. Home remedies and patent medicines filled the gap. Families delayed seeking care until conditions were dire, and some never sought it at all.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 45-50

transportation.narrative: The automobile had transformed plains life by the 1920s. Most farm families owned a Model T or Model A Ford, giving them access to town, neighbors, and church within minutes rather than hours. But the Depression and drought drained the means to operate and maintain a car. Gasoline, tires, and repairs required cash that no longer existed. For families who stayed, the car sat idle or was converted to horse-drawn power — a 'Bennett buggy' or 'Hoover wagon.' For families who left, the car became the migration vehicle: an overloaded Model T or Chevrolet piled with mattresses, cooking pots, children, and whatever possessions could be lashed to the roof and running boards, heading west on Route 66 toward an uncertain future in California.

details.item: Model T and Model A Ford
details.description: By the mid-1920s, automobile ownership was nearly universal among Southern Plains farm families. The Model T Ford (produced through 1927, priced as low as $260) and its successor the Model A ($385-$570) had revolutionized rural life, shrinking distances and connecting isolated farms to towns, markets, and social networks. A farm family with a car could reach town in 30 minutes instead of half a day by wagon.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 15-18

details.description: As farm income collapsed, many families could not afford gasoline at 15 to 20 cents per gallon, nor tires, oil, or mechanical repairs. Cars sat idle in barnyards. Some were converted to horse-drawn vehicles — called 'Bennett buggies' in Canada or 'Hoover wagons' in the United States — by removing the engine and hitching a team to the frame. Others were put up on blocks to preserve the tires, awaiting better times that for many never came.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 130-135

details.description: U.S. Route 66, running from Chicago to Los Angeles through Oklahoma, the Texas panhandle, and New Mexico, became the iconic highway of Dust Bowl migration. Families loaded cars to the breaking point with mattresses, washtubs, water jugs, tools, clothing, and children, and headed west — a journey of 1,200 to 1,500 miles taking a week or more. Flat tires, breakdowns, and running out of money were constant hazards. Migrant camps sprang up along the route.
details.source: Gregory, 'American Exodus,' pp. 40-52

details.description: Families often traveled in groups for mutual aid — sharing tools, spare parts, food, and labor when vehicles broke down. These informal caravans of three to ten cars became a defining image of the migration. Dorothea Lange and other FSA photographers documented the convoys: overloaded vehicles with household goods roped to every surface, children peering from back seats, license plates from Oklahoma, Kansas, Texas, and Arkansas.
details.source: Lange and Taylor, 'An American Exodus,' pp. 60-75

communication.narrative: Communication on the Southern Plains combined old and new technologies. Rural free delivery brought mail and the county newspaper to farm mailboxes. Telephone party lines connected some families, though not all. But the radio — powered by battery where electricity was unavailable — was the transformative medium of the era. In the evenings, families gathered around the radio for news, weather reports, entertainment programs, and President Roosevelt's fireside chats, which spoke directly to their crisis. Government extension agents, dispatched by the Soil Conservation Service after 1935, brought information about contour plowing, terracing, and other conservation techniques that represented a federal response to the disaster.

details.description: Battery-powered radios connected isolated farm families to the wider world during the Depression. Evening listening was a family ritual: news broadcasts, weather reports, Amos 'n' Andy, the Grand Ole Opry, and FDR's fireside chats. Roosevelt's radio addresses, beginning in 1933, spoke directly to the despair of farm families and explained New Deal programs in plain language. The radio was often one of the last possessions a family sold before leaving.
details.source: Gregory, 'American Exodus,' pp. 18-22

details.description: Where telephone service existed in the rural Southern Plains, it operated on party lines shared by four to twelve families. Eavesdropping was universal and expected. The party line served as an informal news network — storm warnings, community events, and family emergencies spread rapidly. Not all farms were connected; installation and monthly service fees of $1 to $3 were beyond many families' means during the Depression.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 20-22

details.description: The weekly county newspaper — typically four to eight pages — reported local news, farm prices, weather, church schedules, government relief information, and who had left for California. These papers are now invaluable historical records of community disintegration, documenting farm auctions, school closings, church dissolutions, and the steady attrition of families. Editors often wrote poignant editorials urging residents to hold on.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 60-65

details.description: Soil Conservation Service agents, deployed beginning in 1935 under Hugh Hammond Bennett's direction, visited farms to promote contour plowing, terracing, strip cropping, and the retirement of marginal land from cultivation. Their work represented both practical agricultural education and a federal acknowledgment that the plow-up of native grassland had been a catastrophic mistake. Some farmers cooperated; others resisted what they saw as government interference.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 190-210

entertainment_and_social_life.narrative: The church was the backbone of Southern Plains community life — Baptist, Methodist, Church of Christ — and it endured even as everything else collapsed. Pie suppers, barn dances, school programs, 4-H clubs, and Grange meetings sustained social bonds through the 1920s and into the early Depression. Radio programs provided evening entertainment when going out was impossible. Movies in town cost ten to twenty-five cents when anyone had cash. But the defining social experience of the Dust Bowl was community disintegration: watching neighbors pack up and leave, attending farewell gatherings that became grimly routine, and wondering whether to stay or go. Those who remained drew closer together in solidarity, sharing labor, food, and emotional support.

details.description: The Protestant church — typically Baptist or Methodist in the Southern Plains — was the institution that held communities together longest. Sunday services, Wednesday prayer meetings, potluck dinners, and revival meetings provided spiritual sustenance and social connection. Preachers addressed the dust storms in biblical terms, some seeing divine judgment, others urging perseverance. As congregations shrank, churches merged or closed, and the loss of a church often marked the death of a community.
details.source: Egan, 'The Worst Hard Time,' pp. 120-128

details.description: Pie suppers — where women baked pies to be auctioned, with the winning bidder sharing the pie with the baker — were popular fundraisers and social events at schoolhouses and churches. Barn dances, square dances, and community sings provided entertainment that required no cash. These gatherings sustained morale and reinforced community bonds during the worst years, offering a few hours of normalcy in a world coming apart.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 25-28

details.description: When dust storms made travel impossible and poverty limited options, the radio became the center of evening family life. Programs like Amos 'n' Andy, Fibber McGee and Molly, the Lone Ranger, and the Grand Ole Opry provided laughter and escape. Children did homework by the radio's glow. For isolated families on failing farms, the radio was sometimes the only connection to a world that seemed to be functioning normally.
details.source: Gregory, 'American Exodus,' pp. 20-22

details.description: The most painful social experience of the Dust Bowl was watching a community die. Families left one by one — sometimes in the night, owing debts they could not pay. School enrollment dropped until schools consolidated or closed. Baseball teams lost players. Church pews emptied. Those who remained felt the weight of each departure. Oral histories record the anguish of choosing between staying on land the family had homesteaded and leaving to save the children from dust pneumonia.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 45-55

household_goods.narrative: The material inventory of a 1920s Southern Plains farmhouse reflected genuine, earned prosperity: a cast-iron or gas cookstove, an icebox or early Frigidaire, a battery radio, a phonograph with records, a treadle or electric sewing machine, upholstered furniture, linoleum floors, curtained windows, and shelves of books and keepsakes. By the mid-1930s, this accumulation of material life was being liquidated for survival. Farm auction sales — where a family's entire possessions were sold on the courthouse steps or in the farmyard — were a weekly occurrence in every county. What a family chose to load onto the car for California tells the story of material life stripped to its essentials: mattresses, cooking pots, blankets, water jugs, tools, family photographs, and the family Bible.

details.description: A prosperous Southern Plains farmhouse in the 1920s contained goods that marked a generation's rise from frontier hardship: a good cookstove (gas in some areas by the late 1920s), an icebox or mechanical refrigerator, a sewing machine, upholstered chairs, iron bedsteads with mattresses, linoleum floor coverings, curtains, dishes, and a phonograph or radio. Some families had Delco home electric plants. This material comfort was the tangible reward of the wheat boom.
details.source: Hurt, 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History,' pp. 10-14

details.description: As mortgages were foreclosed and taxes went unpaid, farm families were forced to sell everything at auction — livestock, equipment, furniture, kitchen goods, clothing, and personal items. Auctioneers traveled a circuit of despair, selling the material lives of families who had spent decades building them. Neighbors sometimes organized 'penny auctions,' bidding tiny amounts and intimidating outside bidders, then returning goods to the original owner — an act of solidarity and defiance.
details.source: Egan, 'The Worst Hard Time,' pp. 225-232

details.item: What went on the car to California
details.description: Families leaving for California could carry only what fit on and in an automobile. Mattresses were roped to the roof. Washtubs, cooking pots, water containers, tools, and blankets were lashed to running boards and fenders. Clothing was packed in suitcases or bundled in sheets. Family photographs, the Bible, and essential documents were kept inside. Everything else — furniture, the sewing machine, the phonograph, books, dishes — was sold, given away, or left behind in the empty house.
details.source: Lange and Taylor, 'An American Exodus,' pp. 80-95

details.description: The sewing machine — typically a Singer treadle model — was among the most critical household tools during the Depression years. It enabled women to remake adult clothing into children's garments, construct flour-sack dresses, repair work clothes, and produce quilts and household textiles without cash expenditure. Families that still had a functioning sewing machine could clothe themselves. Its loss at auction or its abandonment when leaving was a material and emotional blow.
details.source: Gregory, 'American Exodus,' pp. 24-26

details.description: Before the final departure, families sold possessions incrementally to buy food, fuel, and medicine. The phonograph and records went first. Then furniture. Then the good dishes. Then tools. Each sale marked a step down the ladder of material life that the family had spent years climbing. Women's oral histories describe the pain of selling a wedding gift, a mother's china, or a child's toy — small losses that accumulated into the total dissolution of a household.
details.source: Worster, 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s,' pp. 135-142

sensory_snapshot: The farmhouse sits alone on a flat tableland, its white clapboard grayed by years of wind-driven grit. A wall of brown-black cloud fills the northern horizon from ground to sky, advancing with an eerie silence before the wind hits. Then the air turns solid. Dust drives horizontally through gaps around the window frames, depositing a fine tan film across the kitchen table, the stovetop, the plates stacked face-down in the cupboard. A wet sheet nailed over the front door sags inward, its weave darkening with captured soil. The windmill stands motionless — there is no water to pump. Outside, fence posts disappear into drifts of fine dirt, and the barn's leeward wall has a dune building against it that reaches the eaves. The light dims to a deep amber, then to darkness at midday. Static crackles along the barbed-wire fence line with a faint blue glow. The only sound is the wind — a roaring, sand-blasting pressure against every surface — and somewhere inside the house, a child coughing.
narrative_hooks: A family that built a six-room frame house with wheat-boom money in 1925, then watched it fill with dust and sold it at auction for unpaid taxes ten years later — the arc of prosperity to ruin compressed into a single decade.

Wet sheets hung over every window and doorframe, rewetted through the night while a three-day black blizzard raged outside — this was not frontier hardship but the collapse of a modern agricultural world.

The penny auction: neighbors bidding five cents for a man's plow, ten cents for his team, and handing everything back to him while the bank's agent watched helplessly — community solidarity as an act of defiance.
A child's dust mask fashioned from a flour sack, worn to school along a road where the fence posts had disappeared under drifts — the physical reality of growing up in the Dust Bowl.
What went on the car to California: mattresses, cooking pots, water jugs, the family Bible, and one box of photographs — the material residue of a life stripped to what could be carried.

The radio playing FDR's fireside chat in a sealed farmhouse at night, dust sifting onto the table, the family gathered around the only voice that acknowledged their existence — the intersection of technology, politics, and desperation.
source_references: Worster, Donald. 'Dust Bowl: The Southern Plains in the 1930s.' Oxford University Press, 1979.
Egan, Timothy. 'The Worst Hard Time: The Untold Story of Those Who Survived the Great American Dust Bowl.' Houghton Mifflin, 2006.
Gregory, James N. 'American Exodus: The Dust Bowl Migration and Okie Culture in California.' Oxford University Press, 1989.
Hurt, R. Douglas. 'The Dust Bowl: An Agricultural and Social History.' Nelson-Hall, 1981.
Lange, Dorothea and Paul Schuster Taylor. 'An American Exodus: A Record of Human Erosion.' Reynal and Hitchcock, 1939.
